figures.gloss: The Sufi order as institution: a master (shaykh, pir) binding disciples through initiation and an unbroken chain of transmission (silsila) reaching back to the Prophet — Qadiri, Naqshbandi, Chishti, Mevlevi and hundreds more.
figures.facet: Mysticism given a genealogy: ecstatic experience is not free-floating but franchised through lineage, so that a disciple in Delhi or Konya can name every hand between his own and Muhammad's.
figures.provenance: Order histories from al-Jilani (d. 1166, Qadiriyya), Mu'in al-Din Chishti (d. 1236), Baha' al-Din Naqshband (d. 1389); surveyed in Trimingham, The Sufi Orders in Islam, and Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam.

figures.gloss: The rhythmic repetition of God's Name — voiced or silent, breath-tied, sometimes danced — practiced until remembrance displaces the rememberer; the Mevlevi whirling sema is its most famous choreography.
figures.facet: The technology at Sufism's core: not doctrine but a repeatable practice for dismantling the self with a single word, tuned differently by every order (the Naqshbandi silent, the Rifa'i loud).
figures.provenance: Qur'anic injunction to remember God often (e.g., 33:41) as the proof-text; manuals such as al-Qushayri's Risala and Schimmel's treatment of dhikr technique.

figures.gloss: The ecstatic of Baghdad executed in 922 after years of imprisonment, remembered above all for the utterance 'Ana al-Haqq' — 'I am the Truth/Real' — divine union spoken aloud in the marketplace.
figures.facet: The tradition's boundary-stone: later Sufis (Junayd's sober school) judged him not wrong but INDISCREET — the secret of union was true and should have stayed secret; his gallows became a station of the path.
figures.provenance: Louis Massignon, The Passion of al-Hallaj; the trial and execution are historically documented, while the exact circumstances of the 'Ana al-Haqq' utterance rest on later hagiography — flagged.

figures.gloss: The Andalusian 'Greatest Master' (al-Shaykh al-Akbar, 1165-1240), whose Meccan Revelations and Ringstones of Wisdom articulate the vision later named wahdat al-wujud — the oneness of being, all existence as the self-disclosure of the One.
figures.facet: The metaphysical ceiling of the tradition: every thing that exists is a face God shows Himself — though the label wahdat al-wujud is his school's coinage more than his own phrase, a precision his defenders and detractors both blur.
figures.provenance: Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya and Fusus al-Hikam; Chittick, The Sufi Path of Knowledge, including the point that the term wahdat al-wujud is scarcely Ibn 'Arabi's own.

figures.gloss: The Persian poet of Konya (1207-1273) whose meeting with the wandering dervish Shams of Tabriz turned a jurist into the singer of the Masnavi — six books of couplets his followers call 'the Qur'an in Persian' — and whose circle became the Mevlevi order.
figures.facet: Proof that a mystical tradition can conquer through beauty alone: Rumi's verse carried Sufism further than any conquest or mission, into languages and centuries that never saw a lodge.
figures.provenance: Masnavi-yi Ma'navi and Divan-i Shams; Franklin Lewis, Rumi: Past and Present, East and West. The Mevlevi order was institutionalized by his son Sultan Walad.

figures.gloss: The saint whose closeness to God persists after death, sought at tomb-shrines from Ajmer to Touba through visitation (ziyara), offerings, and petitions for intercession — the devotional infrastructure of much of the Islamic world.
figures.facet: Where Sufism meets the ground: the shrine network made the friends of God locally addressable — and made Sufism the prime target of the modern Salafi campaign against 'grave-worship,' from 19th-century polemic to the demolition of shrines in our own decade.
figures.provenance: Classical sainthood doctrine in al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi and Ibn 'Arabi; shrine culture and its Salafi opponents documented across modern ethnography and reportage (e.g., destruction of Sufi shrines in Timbuktu, 2012, tried before the ICC).

figures.gloss: Basran freedwoman-ascetic (c.717-801) remembered as the founding voice of disinterested love-mysticism in Islam, who prayed to love God for God's own sake alone — the hagiographic image has her carrying a torch and a bowl of water to burn Paradise and quench Hellfire so no one would worship from hope or fear.
figures.facet: Anchors the tradition's own period note (d. 801) as its earliest named figure and supplies the roster's only female voice: love as the higher motive that later sober and ecstatic schools alike had to answer to.
figures.provenance: Margaret Smith, 'Rabi'a the Mystic and Her Fellow-Saints in Islam' (1928); the fire-and-water image is transmitted through Farid al-Din 'Attar's 'Tadhkirat al-Awliya,' hagiography rather than a contemporary record.

figures.gloss: Baghdadi jurist-mystic (d. 910) called 'the master of the folk' (sayyid al-ta'ifa), architect of 'sober' Sufism: annihilation (fana) disciplined by a return to subsistence (baqa), the mystical secret kept close, union with God held inside the boundaries of the law rather than proclaimed in the street.
figures.facet: The hinge most silsilas route through and the counterweight built into the tradition's own core: Junayd's school judged al-Hallaj's public utterance not false but indiscreet — the truth of union was never the question, only whether it belonged in a marketplace.
figures.provenance: Junayd's surviving letters and sayings, collected and translated by Ali Hassan Abdel-Kader, 'The Life, Personality and Writings of al-Junayd' (1962); Schimmel, 'Mystical Dimensions of Islam.'

figures.gloss: Jurist-theologian (1058-1111), chief professor at Baghdad's Nizamiyya madrasa, whose spiritual crisis led him to resign his post and take up Sufi practice; his 'Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din' ('Revival of the Religious Sciences') rebuilt Islamic law and theology around interior Sufi discipline.
figures.facet: Arguably the single most consequential event in Sufism's history: after Ghazali, mystical interiority is no longer a suspect fringe practice but installed at the center of mainstream Sunni piety.
figures.provenance: Al-Munqidh min al-Dalal ('Deliverance from Error'), his spiritual autobiography; Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din; Frank Griffel, 'Al-Ghazali's Philosophical Theology' (2009).

figures.gloss: Hanbali preacher of Baghdad (1077-1166) around whose memory the Qadiriyya, the most widespread Sufi order, formed generations after his death; his Baghdad tomb draws worldwide visitation and an annual devotional gathering (the Ghawthiyya).
figures.facet: The paradigm case the roster's wali entry gestures at without naming a person: the Friend of God addressed after death, made locally and globally reachable through shrine, order, and praise-name (al-Ghawth, 'the Help').
figures.provenance: Al-Jilani's own 'Al-Ghunya li-Talibi Tariq al-Haqq' and 'Futuh al-Ghayb'; order history in J. Spencer Trimingham, 'The Sufi Orders in Islam.'

figures.gloss: Persian ecstatic (d. c. 874) of Bistam whose shathiyat ('ecstatic utterances') — 'Glory be to Me, how great is My majesty!' — and a narrated mi'raj-like ascent to God set the pole of 'intoxicated' (sukr) Sufism a generation before al-Hallaj.
figures.facet: The other pole to Junayd's sobriety: where Junayd builds the discipline and al-Hallaj dies at its edge, Bistami states the same utterance-problem first, his words transmitted, like Hallaj's, only through later compilers rather than his own hand.
figures.provenance: Sayings preserved in al-Sarraj's 'Kitab al-Luma'' and al-Sahlaji's 'Kitab al-Nur'; discussed in Schimmel, 'Mystical Dimensions of Islam,' and Carl Ernst, 'Words of Ecstasy in Sufism' (1985).

figures.gloss: Nishapur poet-pharmacist (c.1145 - c.1221) whose 'Mantiq al-Tayr' ('Conference of the Birds') allegorizes the mystic path as thirty birds' quest for the Simurgh, who turns out to be the birds themselves (si morgh, 'thirty birds'), and whose 'Tadhkirat al-Awliya' fixed the hagiographic memory of the earlier saints, al-Hallaj included.
figures.facet: Standing exactly between al-Hallaj and Rumi, he is the hinge who turned scattered oral memory into a canon: without his Tadhkira, much of the tradition's founding hagiography would have no textual anchor at all.
figures.provenance: 'Mantiq al-Tayr' (tr. Afkham Darbandi & Dick Davis as 'The Conference of the Birds'); 'Tadhkirat al-Awliya'; Rumi's own acknowledged debt is recorded by later biographers.

figures.gloss: Wandering dervish whose sudden arrival in Konya and encounter with Jalal al-Din Rumi in 1244 turned a respected jurist into an ecstatic poet; Shams vanished without explanation around 1247, and his recorded talks ('Maqalat-i Shams') survive alongside Rumi's Divan, which bears his name.
figures.facet: Load-bearing and shard-less: his disappearance is as formative to the tradition's memory of Rumi as his arrival, and his entire historical weight is a transformation triggered in someone else.
figures.provenance: 'Maqalat-i Shams-i Tabrizi'; Franklin Lewis, 'Rumi: Past and Present, East and West'; the exact circumstances of Shams's disappearance (murder versus voluntary departure) remain historically disputed — flagged.

figures.gloss: 'The Green One,' identified in tradition with the unnamed servant of God in Qur'an 18:60-82 whose actions defy and out-know Moses' understanding of the law; in Sufi doctrine he is immortal, still walking the earth, and can initiate a seeker directly, without need of a living shaykh's hand.
figures.facet: Sufism's standing exception inside a tradition built on chain-of-hands transmission: the uwaysi channel (named for Uways al-Qarani, said to have received Muhammad's teaching without meeting him) runs through Khidr, authorizing sainthood the silsila cannot itself certify.
figures.provenance: Qur'an 18:60-82 (unnamed in the text, identified as Khidr in tafsir and hadith literature); classical doctrine surveyed in Schimmel, 'Mystical Dimensions of Islam,' and Patrick Franke, 'Begegnung mit Khidr' (2000).

figures.gloss: The complete human being as the mirror in which God beholds the totality of His own names and attributes; Muhammad is its full historical realization and the living saint inherits the station in every age. Systematized as a doctrine's proper name by 'Abd al-Karim al-Jili's treatise 'al-Insan al-Kamil' (c. 1400), building on Ibn 'Arabi.
figures.facet: The doctrinal ceiling above wahdat al-wujud: if all being is God's self-disclosure, the Perfect Man is the one vessel that discloses it completely, folding prophethood and sainthood into a single cosmic office.
figures.provenance: 'Abd al-Karim al-Jili, 'Al-Insan al-Kamil fi Ma'rifat al-Awakhir wa'l-Awa'il'; grounded in Ibn 'Arabi's 'Fusus al-Hikam'; Reynold A. Nicholson, 'Studies in Islamic Mysticism.'

figures.gloss: Classical doctrine of an invisible government of saints upholding the world: the Qutb ('Pole' or 'Axis') at the summit, beneath him the Abdal ('Substitutes') and Awtad ('Pegs'), each office silently refilled from the ranks below whenever its holder dies.
figures.facet: Sainthood organized as cosmic office rather than isolated ecstatic achievement: every wali venerated at a shrine is understood to hold, or to have held, a specific rank in a hierarchy that never stands empty.
figures.provenance: Al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi, 'Khatm al-Awliya'' (the earliest systematic treatment); elaborated by Ibn 'Arabi and later manuals; surveyed in Michel Chodkiewicz, 'Seal of the Saints' (1993).

figures.gloss: The classical manuals' map of the mystical path: maqamat are stations earned through discipline and effort — repentance, patience, trust, poverty, contentment — held stably once attained; ahwal are states given unearned and transient — expansion, contraction, intimacy, awe — that come and go by grace alone.
figures.facet: The tradition's own grammar for what its individual travelers are actually doing: not free-floating ecstasy but progress along, and interruption within, a described and taught itinerary.
figures.provenance: Al-Qushayri, 'Al-Risala al-Qushayriyya' (1046); Abu Nasr al-Sarraj, 'Kitab al-Luma'' — both already cited in the roster's dhikr and tariqa provenance.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Every silsila terminates in the Prophet (most via 'Ali) — Sufism is Islam's interior discipline claiming direct descent, not a separate religion beside it.
GOLD SEAM: Fana, annihilation in the Beloved, is the terminus of dhikr practice — the parent shard already carries Sufism's crown concept; one seam, same body.

GOLD SEAM: The Alevi cem's station of judgment is named dar-i Mansur, 'the gallows of Mansur' — al-Hallaj's execution ritually re-lived in Anatolia, a documented borrowing of the martyr into the liturgy.
GOLD SEAM: Haci Bektas was a Khorasani Sufi pir and the Bektashi order bearing his name is itself a tariqa — Alevism grows from Sufi rootstock, though the degree of continuity is debated.

GOLD SEAM: The Guru Granth Sahib canonizes verses attributed to the Chishti master Shaykh Farid — a Sufi voice bound inside Sikh scripture; the inclusion is fact, though whether the hymns are Farid's own or his Pakpattan successors' is debated.
GOLD SEAM: Mevlevi sema and Alevi semah — two ecstatic turning rites of the same Anatolian soil and era; kinship of milieu and vocabulary, not proven descent.

GOLD SEAM: Breath-tied repetition of the divine Name until the heart prays itself — the Jesus Prayer and dhikr are the classic comparanda of contemplative technique; mutual influence often suggested, never proven.

Cross-tradition: Amazigh saintly lineages (igurramen, or marabouts) are locally embedded wali — hereditary baraka-holders operating within, not merely parallel to, Islamic Sufi sainthood doctrine (Gellner).
bead-counted, orally repeated devotion as a technology for holding attention through repetition rather than varying content.
In Sufi hagiography Khidr initiates saints directly, unbound by any human silsila — the one widely attested exception to the chain of transmission the tariqa otherwise requires.
The Names are dhikr's primary content — reciting and counting them is the standard form the remembrance practice takes.
Intra-tradition bond: her hagiographic image and sayings survive chiefly through Attar's 'Tadhkirat al-Awliya.'
Intra-tradition bond: the judge of sobriety and the disciple he judged not wrong but indiscreet — the tradition's own founding argument about how much of the secret may be spoken.
Intra-tradition bond: the sober school and the intoxicated pole it defines itself against, the two named schools of classical Sufi psychology.
Intra-tradition bond: eponym of the Qadiriyya, the most widespread order the tariqa entry surveys.
Intra-tradition bond: the paradigm case behind the shrine-and-intercession entry, named at last.
Intra-tradition bond: the earlier ecstatic utterance that set the problem al-Hallaj died on a generation later.
Intra-tradition bond: Rumi's own acknowledged poetic precursor, recorded by later biographers.
Intra-tradition bond: Attar's 'Tadhkirat al-Awliya' is the text that fixed Hallaj's passion into the tradition's hagiographic canon.
Intra-tradition bond: the 1244 encounter that turned a jurist into the poet of the Masnavi.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: widely documented Levantine folk identification of Khidr with Elijah — shared immortality, shared shrines, the two treated in places as one figure under two names.
Intra-tradition bond: the doctrine is built directly out of, and systematized after, Ibn 'Arabi's wahdat al-wujud.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the perfected primal human as the cosmic mirror in which the divine attributes are fully disclosed — structural analogy, not shared descent.
Intra-tradition bond: the organized hierarchy standing behind every individual wali the shrine entry describes.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: an organized company of the sanctified dead and living upholding and interceding for the world — structural parallel between two independently developed sainthood doctrines.
Intra-tradition bond: the practice the roster already describes, plotted along the very stations and states this scheme names.
ecstatic mystics who made public, danced or chanted devotional performance — sankirtana, sama — the highest practice of the age, independently of each other.

dossier.essence: The mystical-ascetic dimension of Islam, practiced within both Sunni and Shi'i communities from West Africa to Southeast Asia and now globally. It seeks direct experiential knowledge of God through purification of the self, remembrance, and love, and since the 12th-13th centuries has been organized into initiatory orders (tariqas) whose chains of transmission (silsila) reach back to the Prophet Muhammad.
dossier.cosmos: Sufism keeps the standard Islamic cosmology — one God, creation, prophecy, judgment, paradise and hell — but reads it inwardly: the world is the theater of God's self-disclosure, and the soul's real journey is a path of stations and states culminating in annihilation of the ego (fana) and subsistence in God (baqa), tasted before death rather than only after it. In the school of Ibn 'Arabi this vision reaches its metaphysical ceiling: all existence as the self-disclosure of the One, a doctrine his followers named wahdat al-wujud.
dossier.practice: The disciple binds to a master (shaykh, pir) through initiation and practices dhikr — voiced, silent, breath-tied, or danced repetition of God's Name, most famously choreographed in the Mevlevi whirling sema — alongside the ordinary obligations of Islamic law. Retreats, poetry and ritual audition (sama), and visitation (ziyara) of saints' tomb-shrines fill out the devotional life; orders such as the Qadiri, Naqshbandi, Chishti, and Mevlevi span continents, and the shrine network has made Sufism the prime target of modern Salafi polemic and iconoclasm.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an and hadith read esoterically are the bedrock, elaborated in a classical manual literature (al-Qushayri's Risala, al-Ghazali's Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din) and in monuments such as Ibn 'Arabi's al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya and Rumi's Masnavi.
dossier.reflects: The interiorization of a law-religion — the insistence that revelation exists to be tasted, engineered through repeatable technique and franchised through lineage rather than left to spontaneous grace.
sources: J. Spencer Trimingham, 'The Sufi Orders in Islam' (1971)
William C. Chittick, 'The Sufi Path of Knowledge' (1989)

links.cite: J. Spencer Trimingham, 'The Sufi Orders in Islam' (1971)
William C. Chittick, 'The Sufi Path of Knowledge' (1989)
